seo.page_title: Import petrol cars from Japan to NZ
seo.meta_description: Import petrol cars from Japan with clear landed pricing, auction reports before you bid, and NZ-based support from search to delivery.
seo.og_title: Import petrol cars from Japan to NZ
seo.og_description: Looking at a petrol import? We help you compare options, read auction sheets and understand landed costs before you decide to bid.

blocks.hero_subtitle: Bring in a petrol car from Japan with clear landed pricing, auction reports before you bid, and a NZ team that talks you through each step.
proof_points: Transparent pricing — compliance is part of the process
blocks.why_kiwis_buy: Many Kiwis import petrol cars from Japan to get more choice, lower mileage and better features than local stock. It works for everything from first cars to family wagons and utes. You set the budget and we help match it to what’s realistic at auction.

savings_block.headline: Where the money goes — from Japan auction to NZ road-ready
savings_block.body: For a typical petrol import, you start with the Japanese auction price, then add shipping, marine insurance, NZ port charges, entry certification, compliance work and our service fee to reach the landed cost. We lay out these numbers in advance so you can see how different auction prices affect the final figure. That way you can decide what you’re comfortable bidding before we go near an auction.

cta_form.headline: Find your petrol import — we'll do the searching
cta_form.subtext: Fill in your details to get started. We'll search the Japanese auctions and come back with real options and pricing by email.

blocks.video_caption: In this walkaround we show a typical Japanese petrol import, including interior condition, engine bay and how to read key parts of the auction sheet.
blocks.main_usp_deep: Bringing in a petrol car from Japan opens up a wide range of body styles and engine sizes, often with lower mileage than similar cars already in NZ. We focus on clean auction sheets, sensible odometer readings and service records where they’re available. You see the documentation before we bid, so you can weigh up condition against price. For most buyers, that balance of choice and value is the main reason to import rather than buy locally.

buyer_persona.headline: Is this car right for you?
buyer_persona.body: You might be after a first car, a daily commuter, or a practical family runabout and don’t want to overpay. You’re comfortable waiting a few weeks if it means getting better value and more choice. If you like clear numbers and straight answers, importing a petrol car through us is a solid match.

trust_company.headline: Why import with ImportYourCar.co.nz?
trust_company.body: ImportYourCar.co.nz has been helping New Zealanders bring in cars from Japan since 2009. We’re a NZ-based team that works directly with the major Japanese auctions and exporters. You get full auction documentation, photos and our notes before you decide to bid. From first enquiry through to compliance and handover, we keep the process transparent so you always know what stage your car is at.

car_specs_benefits.benefit: Choose from smaller, efficient engines for commuting through to larger units better suited to towing or long trips.
Easy to drive in traffic, with smoother take-off and less effort on hills and around town.

car_specs_benefits.spec: Front-wheel drive and all-wheel drive variants
car_specs_benefits.benefit: Front-wheel drive keeps running costs down, while all-wheel drive can give more confidence on wet roads and gravel.
Many late-model imports include stability control, multiple airbags and driver aids that help with NZ open-road and city driving.
From compact hatchbacks to people movers and utes, you can match the car to your family, work or hobby needs.

cta_mid_page.headline: Start your Japanese petrol car search
cta_mid_page.subtext: Tell us what you’re after and we’ll come back with real auction examples and landed cost estimates.

models_comparison.intro: Most buyers narrow their search to a few petrol models before we start bidding.

rows.notes: Later years usually mean more safety tech and lower mileage but higher auction prices. We also check that the build year meets current NZ entry rules, including ESC requirements for light vehicles.
Petrol imports can range from small-displacement engines aimed at economy through to larger units with more power. We explain how each option affects fuel use, road tax and day-to-day running costs.
Higher trims may add features like better seats, climate control, parking sensors or upgraded audio. We point out which features matter in NZ and which are mainly cosmetic.
We look for ESC, airbag and structural notes on the auction sheet and check that the car typically meets NZ entry standards if documentation is clean and no modifications are noted.
Common models appear daily and give more choice on colour, mileage and grade. Rarer models may take longer to find and can attract stronger bidding at auction.

steps.title: Tell us what you're after
steps.body: Fill in the form or message us. We confirm your brief and give you a cost breakdown before anything moves forward.

steps.title: We search the auctions
steps.body: We monitor USS, TAA, HAA and others. For petrol cars, we pay close attention to engine notes, oil leaks, underbody comments and any mention of modifications on the auction sheet.

steps.title: You approve, we bid
steps.body: We send you the auction sheet and our recommendation. Once you're happy, you pay a deposit and we bid on your behalf.

steps.title: Shipping to NZ
steps.body: Typically 2–4 weeks depending on the sailing schedule. For less common models, allow a little extra time once the auction clears. We keep you updated along the way.

steps.title: Compliance and delivery
steps.body: The car goes through NZ entry certification and compliance. Once it's through, we arrange delivery or you can collect.

risks_solutions.intro: Importing a petrol car from Japan is straightforward, but it helps to know what to expect.

items.risk: Worrying about buying a car you haven’t seen in person.
items.solution: We base our advice on the Japanese auction sheet, photos and, where available, service records. You see these documents and our comments before you decide whether to bid.

items.risk: Concern about hidden costs on top of the auction price.
items.solution: Before bidding, we outline the expected landed cost, including auction price estimate, shipping, insurance, port charges, entry certification, compliance work and our fee, so you can budget properly.

items.risk: Uncertainty about auction grades and what they really mean.
items.solution: We explain the grading system in plain language and highlight any notes about repairs, paintwork, interior wear or mechanical issues so you can judge condition against price.

blocks.how_to_use_iyc: You tell us what sort of petrol car you’re after, your budget and how you’ll use it. We shortlist suitable cars from the Japanese auctions, explain the documentation and landed costs, then only bid once you’re comfortable. If you’d like to explore options, send us a brief and we’ll come back with examples.

faq_block.q: What types of petrol cars can I import from Japan?
faq_block.a: You can import a wide range of petrol vehicles, from small hatchbacks and sedans through to SUVs, people movers and some utes. We’ll help you narrow it down based on budget, size, features and how you plan to use the car.

faq_block.q: How much does it cost to import a petrol car from Japan?
faq_block.a: Total cost depends on the auction price, shipping, marine insurance, NZ port charges, entry certification, compliance work and our service fee. We provide a landed cost estimate before bidding so you can see how different auction prices affect your budget.

faq_block.q: Will my imported petrol car meet NZ compliance standards?
faq_block.a: We target cars that, based on auction documentation, usually meet NZ entry standards if documentation is clean and no modifications are noted. Final approval is made at NZ entry certification once the car is physically inspected.

faq_block.q: How long does it take to get a petrol car from Japan to NZ?
faq_block.a: Once we win an auction, shipping to NZ typically takes 2–4 weeks depending on the sailing schedule and port. Allow extra time for export processing in Japan, NZ entry certification and any compliance work needed.

faq_block.q: What condition are Japanese petrol imports usually in?
faq_block.a: Condition varies by age, mileage and use. Auction grades and comments give a good indication of exterior, interior and mechanical state. We talk you through the sheet, highlight any red flags and only recommend bidding on cars where the condition matches your expectations and budget.

footer_cta.headline: Ready to explore a petrol import from Japan?
footer_cta.subtext: Tell us what you’re looking for and we’ll show you real auction options, documentation and landed cost estimates before you decide to bid.

subscription_block.headline: Stay in the loop
subscription_block.subtext: Get updates on Japanese petrol auction trends, example landed costs and new import guides for NZ buyers.

blocks.company_positioning: ImportYourCar.co.nz is a NZ-based import service that’s been helping buyers bring in vehicles from Japan since 2009. We focus on clear landed pricing, full auction documentation and practical advice on compliance and condition. Our role is to help you make an informed decision before any bidding happens.

landed_cost_breakdown.intro: Your landed cost covers the journey from winning the auction in Japan through to a car that’s ready for NZ roads.

rows.notes: The hammer price you pay at the Japanese auction, plus local auction fees in Japan.
Ocean freight from Japan to NZ and marine insurance to cover the vehicle while it’s in transit.
NZ port charges, entry certification, inspections and any compliance work needed to get the car road legal.

auction_checklist.check: Auction grade and any repair history notes
auction_checklist.why: Shows overall condition and whether the car has had panel repairs or structural work that could affect long-term durability or compliance.

auction_checklist.check: Odometer reading and consistency with records
auction_checklist.why: Helps you judge wear and tear and compare value between similar cars at auction.
Notes about oil leaks, noise or shifting issues can point to upcoming mechanical costs.
Underbody condition affects compliance and long-term reliability, especially if the car has lived in snow regions in Japan.
Tells you how the car has been treated and whether it has the comfort and safety features you want for NZ use.

not_for_you_block.headline: Who this may not suit
items: If you need a car immediately and can’t wait for shipping and compliance, a local purchase may be easier.
If you’re uncomfortable making decisions from photos and documentation, importing might feel too hands-off.
If you want very specific colours or rare trims, you may need to be flexible on timing and budget.

alternatives_block.intro: If a petrol import doesn’t quite line up with your needs, there are other paths to consider.

rows.better_if: You want lower fuel use and do a lot of city driving.
rows.tradeoff: Hybrid systems can be more complex and may have higher repair costs if something fails out of warranty.

rows.better_if: You prefer local service history and being able to see the car in person before buying.
rows.tradeoff: You’ll usually pay more for similar age and mileage compared with a Japanese import.

rows.better_if: You tow regularly or cover high open-road mileage.
rows.tradeoff: Diesel road user charges and potential emissions-related maintenance need to be factored into running costs.

engine_drivetrain_block.headline: Engines and drivetrains: what to look for
engine_drivetrain_block.body: Japanese petrol imports cover a wide range of engine sizes, from small-displacement units aimed at economy through to larger engines better suited to towing or long trips. We look closely at auction notes for oil leaks, timing belt or chain comments, transmission behaviour and any mention of modifications. This helps you avoid cars with obvious mechanical red flags before you commit to bidding.
checklist: Confirm whether the engine uses a timing belt or chain and when it was last serviced, if recorded.
Check auction notes for oil leaks, engine noise or smoke on start-up.
Review transmission comments for slipping, harsh shifts or warning lights.
Look for any mention of aftermarket modifications to intake, exhaust or suspension.
Check drivetrain layout (FWD, RWD, AWD) matches how and where you plan to drive.

category_block.headline: Japanese petrol imports for NZ buyers
category_block.body: This segment covers most petrol-powered cars you can import from Japan, from compact city hatches to larger family SUVs and people movers. Rather than focusing on a single model, we help you compare a few options that suit your budget and how you drive. That way you can decide whether to prioritise fuel use, space, comfort or performance.
